speaker: interviewer
text: --after the liberation, when the family came back to Hajdunanas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Absolutely nothing. Resentment. Hate. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't like that we came back. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What had happened to Jewish property, to the shul that you described before, all those things.

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul was there. One of the shul-- there was another one, an older one that was broken down. It was just the walls. Maybe a few steps was left on the side, [INAUDIBLE]. This, for myself, I don't remember before the war. I just saw that it was broken down. So it must have something had done to it. The shul itself, the whole property itself, it's-- it was there, [INAUDIBLE] standing. It was in good shape. The houses, what the Jewish people owned, it was there, and but it was empty. We didn't have any furniture. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And how-- the way we got some food was through the Joint. That's from the American Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --people. They were-- they were giving us something, some clothes, some shoes, some-- whatever they sent, they were giving us-- and some cans, food, canned food, and-- But at one point, somebody find out that one of-- one piece of our bedroom set is at-- by somebody, by a Hungarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my mother goes-- because my mother didn't want to take anybody's furniture, because there was some furnitures pushed into some houses, Jewish houses. What the Hungarians didn't take, they just pushed broken-- some of them broken, some of them were in good condition, but just all piled up, pushed in. And she didn't want to take anybody's. She says, maybe some people still come back, and you know, it's their furniture. But somebody said there is a piece. And this was a very nice piece. And I remember this piece, because I used to like to play with it. It was like a mirror which has two, like, night tables. It looked like a night tables-- two side. And you could open the door. And then, it had-- and the mirror was coming out high. Quite high. And then, the mirror, you could close it in like a book. And then, between these two cupboards-- little night tables, I should say-- like a little shelf and a little drawer to pull out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was very pretty. And I liked to sit on it, look myself in the-- and I just was imagining and playing. And this was my mother's. So my mother went, walked out to almost-- quite far from the-- a distance she had to walk. She walked there. She never knew those people before. So when she went there, and we're all excited that we are going to get back something of ours. Time passes. And she comes back. She's walking slow. She is crying. She is crying. And we ask, what's the matter? Why are you crying? What's wrong?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, don't anybody ever tell me that you find something of mine. I don't care who has it, I don't want it. I'm not going to go for it. What-- and she could hardly talk. She was sobbing. It-- she could hardly talk. It was just awful to see this mother cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, she says to us, when this woman opened-- this Hungarian woman opened the door, and she says, what do you want? And she says, well, I'm so and so. And I-- you have my piece.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother says, when she was opening the door, this woman-- she saw. She saw the furniture a little bit. It's there. So she said, you know, you have this mirror furniture-- because this is one piece, the whole thing was one piece-- and this is mine. And I would like to have it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: This woman started to Jew her down, called her every kind of horrible names, just didn't hit her. So my mother says, after all the suffering, everything what I went through, and I am not OK yet, and for a piece of furniture, I'm going to put myself through again such a horror? She says, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even if I would sleep on the floor and everybody else, no furniture. That's the way we are going to live. I'm not going to go to anybody anymore. So that's what we find when we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you like, physically? What was your strength like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physical, we weren't so OK. Physical, we were pretty run down, very skinny, bony, especially one of my younger brothers. He was-- his suffering with that hunger, it just unbelievable. When he was hungry in the lager, you could see through his ear-- this part of the ear. But completely, like a-- like a pink light, reddish-pink light. And the saliva was coming from his mouth. That's how hungry he was. And when we got a little bit of food-- and we were all very, very hungry-- the first thing all of us, me too, the first bite, Tibor got it, because the-- because the way he suffered, nobody-- and we all suffered, but his was terrible. It was heartbreaking to see him. And I, myself, I was-- I had open wounds. And I had such a pus coming out, mostly from the joints of my body. And this time already, the Joint supply that's also for-- you know, that we can go to this doctor--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a medic?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we can go to this doctor, and this doctor was charging for-- you know, the Joint paid him. That we came back, we needed medical care. So he-- he-- he came to see me, and he says, this is all lack of vitamin, lack of food, lack of nourishment. And that's why I have this. And I had this for many months, until it cleared up. That's how-- that's how life started.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many people did come back that you had remembered? Or how many-- it's hard to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many-- well, very few people came back. No cousins, whatsoever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One of the Safar brother, my grandfather's brother, Herman Safar, amongst his eight children, only one survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the whole family was wiped out. And there was another brother who was also-- he had quite a few children. Only two survived. And there was another brother there. He had seven children. Two survived. So many, many of them. And my father's sister, who was engaged to be married-- she was 21 years old-- and my father's parents, did not survive. And my mother's two brothers and a wife-- one was married, his wife-- didn't survive. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the parents did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were all murdered. They were not-- they didn't die, they were murdered.

speaker: interviewer
text: They had all died at Auschwitz? Mostly?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed in Auschwitz, where they were gassed. My grandfather was gassed. That's-- that's not dying, that was murder.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Were you-- were you aware of the plan that got you to Austria? Did you ever hear about that and how it happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Afterwards, we heard that there was some trains bought. Eichman was dealing with Kastner, with some Hungarian. This is what we heard. This, I cannot tell you, because this is what I just heard afterward. And but we know there are trains supposed to end up in Auschwitz. And we knew that we were on it for six days. And the train, at one time, turned back to Debrecen. And then we were rerouted. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you think that this may have been part of Rezso Kastner's plan with Eichman?

speaker: interviewee
text: it-- it could be, or-- we didn't go to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know for sure. I don't know the history of this part, but this is what we heard. And I-- I-- there were trains, which was bought already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They could have bought everything. They could have stopped us. They didn't have to send us. In 1944, they were losing the war already. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was just they gave us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going, do you think? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The strength of your family? Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably it's my mother. For sure, it's my mother, and probably our faith, our-- our-- our religion, believing in God. Always believed in God. Never for a second I wavered. Even when I was angry, even when I was yelling up to Him, I did not waver. I believed in Him. I always believed in Him. We always. It's just-- it's-- and human life is precious.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you try to do then, when you were back-- you were eight, seven or eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was already eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Eight. I'm sure. Eight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened over the next few years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, I had to recuperate. I had to get my strength back. I had to get these boils cleared up. Because in some joints, I was so bad, I couldn't walk. I had such big boils there. And-- and-- I had to be fed. And I had to be-- my body had to be-- be strengthened up a little. And then I started school. And then I had the hope that father is going to return in a few years. And then that-- that hope was lost. That was a terrible let down. It-- heartbreaking. And then, seeing my mother suffering terrible that she's alone. Although, the children there, but she was alone. She was alone. She didn't have a partner. And then she had let the other children go, because she really couldn't keep-- you know, she just couldn't make enough money to-- to keep us all. So my sister went to Debrecen, to a Jewish home where they taught her to-- a trade. And eventually, this Jewish-- this is a girls home, it was, they escaped from Hungary. They went to Israel. And then, my brother-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one brother went to Budapest. He learned a trade there. And my other brother went to a-- like a [YIDDISH], in different places. Wasn't such a-- also wasn't-- it was-- was quite hard. The-- the accommodation wasn't good, and the food still wasn't good. It wasn't like here in North America. And then, we were growing, and we got older. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you stayed with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always stayed with my mother. My mother-- well, one child, she wouldn't let go. At one point, I said, how come you don't let me go? I said, they are-- they are having it good. I said, they are living in a home, and they have everything, and they learn. So she says, no, one child I have to have. I said, it's hard enough. But they always came home for the holidays. Everybody was together on the holidays. But my brother, my oldest brother, and my other-- first, when he-- he-- he learned his trade, and then he started to make some money, almost everything he gave to my mother. A little bit, he left for himself. My mother was always saying, it's not enough what you leave for yourself. No. But he felt that he has to give that to my mother. Then my other brother, he started to-- you know, he grew up, and he learned a trade. And he also brought home money. And my mother, she was working. She was sewing. And financially, it wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, we had to count every penny, but we didn't complain. It was nothing to complain about anyway, financially. I mean, we didn't have money to go for a holiday or-- or go to another town and visit, but you know, there in [INAUDIBLE], it was, for two of us, it was OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel safe as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Not never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never felt safe as a Jew there. I was always, always, very scared. Where I-- first, when I started school-- it was the Jewish school-- one of the Jewish teacher, [INAUDIBLE]-- I can't remember her last name, [INAUDIBLE]-- she was in Auschwitz with my aunt. She survived. And when she came back, she started school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was like this. In one row, there was maybe one or two children grade one. The second row, grade two. The other row, grade three. But always, just one or two or three children in each class. So when she was teaching the first class, all the other class has to be very quiet. They had to read or do some work. They-- they weren't allowed to talk. And then, in each class, she was going row by row. That's how she was teaching us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, but then, this stopped. They want-- she went away, and then I went to public school. In public school, I-- I-- I wasn't feeling really there, because I was called there, the Judah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I was, at one point, one of the girls told me in the class-- interrupted me-- she says, you dirty Jew. You killed Jesus. I said, how could I kill Jesus? He was 2,000 years ago. I wasn't even alive. No, you killed. You have to suffer. So I never felt safe. What was happening in Hungary at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hungary, there was communism. And under the communism, we didn't have freedom. If we want to go somewhere, we had to show-- we had a book, a little red book, where all our name, all the documentation, what they needed, like a passport. It's a passbook, it was. You had to show it. And all-- you had to carry it with you, and especially if you went out of town. If they wanted-- somebody wanted to show who you are, so you had to show that book. So that had to be carried. It showed that you are a Jew. It-- the religion was put in. Wasn't necessary, but they put it in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what was happening then that made you decide to leave at some point?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, the revolution happened. October came, and the Hungarian Revolution came. And the Hungarian revolution, it came-- it started in Budapest, we heard it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's '56.

speaker: interviewee
text: '56. And they were fighting. There was nothing to it. What I-- what I understood, they were fighting, they don't want the Russians anymore, their communism. I don't know too much about this thing, politics, but this is what I understood. But in Hajdunanas, it's different story happened. This, what I remember, a Friday. I don't know the date, but it was on a Friday. On Friday, I remember I went to the store. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this-- first, on Friday, it was in the afternoon, quite early in the afternoon. I think it was afternoon. There was a big noise. They go to the city hall, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Hungarians. They break everything open. They throw all the documents out. Everything was outside. And then, it was quiet. And then, later in the afternoon, I went to the store. After all, the Sabbath is coming. So I wanted to buy, I don't know, something I wanted to buy. And I'm standing in line. There are big line ups. Silence. You can't hear-- if a bird were to fly, you could hear-- we could have heard the wing of the bird. But everything was silent. And I'm standing there, and I'm very scared. Nobody talks to me. And suddenly, somebody gets a hold of me, in my arm, he holds me. And he pulls me, and he says, you shouldn't stand here. You have no business standing here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, but Mr. Pazman, I'm only standing here because I want to buy this something. And he says, you have no business, and you are not needing it, and you go home and close the door. I said, but why? He says, ask me no questions, now, go home. So the fear, of course, was working. And I went home. And I go home, and my mother is cleaning for Sabbath, the floor, the candles on the table. The challah was there. And I said to my mother, look, something is happening in the city, I said, and you are-- what are you doing? Why are you scrubbing the floor? Maybe we cannot stay here tonight. She says, just don't say anything. Sabbath is coming, and we are going to prepare like other times. And I tell her, I said, get up. Get up. Stop it. I said, maybe we have to run away. I said, and you want to leave them a clean floor? And she was just cleaning. She was doing her business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the evening comes. We have our dinner. We light the candles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And actually, we never leave the candles toward the street, always toward the back yard, so it shouldn't be so noticeable.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- we didn't finish supper yet-- a terrible noise, an awful noise. Scary. And something in this dark street, because now, Hajdunanas was a very dark city. Small city, it was. Not everywhere was light. It wasn't lit up, you know? In Austria, it was very dark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly, you could see light. Light all over. And they came in with their torches. They came with the torches.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, they started to blame the Jews again. So they reached the melamed, the Hebrew teacher, who lost a wife with five children in Auschwitz, remarried an Auschwitz survivor who was-- was-- I don't know. They were doing some experiment on her with limping. She had children-- I don't know, four or five. Poor as a church mouse. The Jewish people-- in-- in-- every week, we put together some money, so they should be able to buy for themselves, you know, like a chicken, or a duck, or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: This Friday night?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this-- I'm coming to that. What we have done. When this screaming was going, this light was coming and this terrible noise, I said to my mother, let's get out of here. But when I-- when we said this, and we said we are going to go out, our neighbor, who was living-- the Liebermans were living in the next door apartment there. So she-- oh. She-- they came over. And she says, something terrible with her husband and her children. She-- she came over, and she said, something terrible is happening. And she was-- and they were sitting there. And then this light was going from the torches. And the noise was coming. And then they jumped up. She says, we have to do something. We go for-- maybe somebody can help us. And they were running. And they leaped through this fence, this wooden fence. And the little boy, the twin boy, he couldn't. And they were in such hurry. And we wanted to lift him up, give over there. But they didn't wait, and he was left with us. I said to my mother, we are not going to go back in the apartment. I am scared. We're just going to go-- we're just-- somewhere, we hide in this yard, somewhere. So she says, but the door is open. I said, never mind the door. They can open it anyhow. They can break in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she insisted, the door has to be closed. So I said, OK, we'll go back, and we are going to close the door. So I take the key. My hands are shaking like this. And I'm trying to put the key in the keyhole, the key.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, it doesn't go. And I said to myself-- I said to my mother, you know, I locked it already. And of course, it wasn't locked. And we were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the outhouse, what was-- where-- where to run. We went to the-- and when I went to the outhouse, I had a scissor in my hand, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because when the first time we ran out from the house, I grabbed the scissor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had this little boy with us, and he is crying and crying. And I said, look, you cannot cry because, if they hear you cry, they will come and get us. Be quiet. He didn't want to be quiet, so I had to really be very strict with him. And I told him, if you won't be quiet, I don't know what I'm doing, but you will be very sorry. So he got scared, and he was sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then, my mother-- and we heard the noise was coming closer and closer. And the melamed's wife, she started to scream. We heard her say, what do you want from this old, broken man? What did he do to you? He's only a poor teacher. Leave him alone. Leave him alone. She's screaming, but it wasn't a human voice anymore, is the way she was screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: To hear this through. It-- it was-- to describe this feeling, this fear, this helplessness, I cannot put it into words. I can't. No matter how I search for words, I cannot put it into words. Then, I said-- and it's coming closer and closer, this horrible noise. And the lights comes. And we're sure they're going to come in. So this time, I said to my mother, we are going to say goodbye to each other. So we said goodbye to each other. We kissed each other. We hugged each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to my mother-- I said, we will see each other in the other world. I said, you know, I want to tell you something. I have a scissor in my hand. You see this? I tell you something, if they want to get me, I have to hurt somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Somebody's going to get one stab from me. I can't take it anymore. I cannot take them anymore. They cannot hurt me anymore. I have done nothing, nothing to no one. Just because of my religion-- I-- it's-- I-- I'm not going to let myself to be killed without me hurting somebody. Mother said nothing. And then, everything quiet-- this was a long time. And finally, everything quieted down. It was pitch black. Everything. And slowly, slowly, we came out from this outhouse. And then, the Liebermans were coming. And they said they find a place. She-- that woman was a-- a woman doctor. She says she's going to-- she's willing to hide us. Because even though in the bad and the evil, there are always some people who will help you. Very few. It's like a drop in the ocean, but there are some. So we went there. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jews were hidden then?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jews were-- we don't know. The rest of the Jews, we didn't know. Everybody was running, left and right. Wherever they could, they run. They were running.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went with the Lieberman family, slept in one farm house. But what happened, we have to go out in the morning. Food wasn't given. We were in our clothes. The food wasn't given. So we were sleeping on the floor, on the bed.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we had to go out the next day, because we had to go to the washrooms. It was our only outhouses in Hajdunanas. And then, the neighbors were seeing us. And this couple who was-- let us in for that night, she says she's very scared. The neighbors are going to say something. And she's scared, so we have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went back to the Lieberman's apartment. It was in the morning. But in the daytime, quiet, quiet. Starting to come the afternoon, it's starting to get dark. And again, we are-- we are very scared. And we are saying, what shall we do? What shall we do? What shall we say? What shall we do? We were afraid they will come in to do the same thing again. So I'm sitting on a very low chair. And I thought that there is no solution. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my body's starting to jump like this. I'm sitting, and I'm jumping. And my mother says, why don't you sit still? I said, I don't know why. I don't know what's happening to me. And my body was jumping, just like that. My nerves. So finally, there was another couple. They had no children. She was a dressmaker. And she said we can come there. So we stayed there with her for two weeks, we were in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was very nice to us. She was very good to us, to do this. She says she's not scared. And after two weeks, what happened, it's already they were losing the revolution also. It wasn't so successful, so the Hungarians quieted down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when you decided--

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I said to my mother, I will not stay here for anything. She says, but we have this, now this. I said, you can stay. And I said it to her, you can stay, because I knew, if I leave, she's going to come after me. I said, but I am going. There is no way I'm going to stay here. I don't feel safe here. I'm scared. This is not a life, and I'm going. And I went up to Budapest. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] By the time I reached-- I don't think I was in Vienna yet-- my mother came up to Budapest too. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for you to show some photos of--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- oh. This is my grandfather, my grandmother, my mother, and I am sitting on my mother's lap, my brother, my father, my other brother, my sister. And that young man is there, my Uncle Moishe. This is my aunt on the other end. And on the other end, my mother's sisters. This is my Rosenberg grandmother, my father's-- father's mother. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my Aunt Margot, my father's sister. She was 21 years old. Engaged to be married, but they killed her in Auschwitz. And this is one of my cousins, this child. I don't know her name. I don't know which one is it. And she was murdered in Auschwitz too.
